title: Meningitis Vaccines for Teenagers in Kent
summary: In Kent, England, some Year 11 students at four schools are getting meningitis B vaccines to keep them safe. This is because there was an outbreak of meningitis, a serious illness, earlier this month. Two people died from it, and others got very sick. Health officials are giving vaccines to 15 and 16-year-olds as a precaution to protect them for a longer time. No new cases have been reported for two days, which is good news. Many people have already received vaccines and antibiotics to stop the spread. The outbreak mostly affected young people who went to a club or live at a university. Students are worried, so they are getting vaccinated to stay healthy.

background_read: Meningitis is a dangerous infection that can cause swelling around the brain and spinal cord. It spreads through close contact, like coughing or sharing drinks. Vaccines help prevent it by teaching the body to fight the germs. In Kent, there was a recent outbreak where young people got sick, so health workers are giving vaccines to teenagers to stop more people from getting ill. This is important because meningitis can be life-threatening, and quick action saves lives.
Article_Structure: WHO: Year 11 pupils at four schools in Kent
WHAT: Getting meningitis B vaccines as a precaution
WHERE: In Kent, England, especially at schools and a club in Canterbury
WHY: To ensure longer-term protection after an outbreak that caused deaths and illnesses

perspectives.description: They are providing vaccines and antibiotics to control the outbreak and protect public health.
They are worried about getting sick and are getting vaccinated to stay safe, with some describing their campus as empty due to fear.
